segments.s065-g17: ترا مژده is a fixed older/poetic phrasing meaning “I give you glad tidings” where ترا is the enclitic 2nd‑person object pronoun (ت) plus the object marker (را) written separately; مژده is the noun “good news.” از introduces the source/subject of the news (دختِ مهراب و زال = “the daughter of Mehrab and Zal”), with دخت (daughter) followed by the proper name مهراب and then the coordinating و and زال.
segments.s065-g18: که introduces a subordinate content clause (“that …”). باشند is the classical third‑person plural present/subjunctive form of بودن used here to state what will be/come to pass (“they will be” / “shall be”). هر دو is the idiom “both (of them). به شادی is an adverbial phrase “in/with joy.” همال here functions as a predicate meaning roughly “matched/peers/companions in bearing,” used poetically; the whole clause says they will be matched together in joy.
segments.s065-g19: ازین is the archaic/poetic form of از + این meaning “from these,” with ین here the orthographic variant of این. هنرمند is used as an epithet ‘skillful/skillful people’ (plural sense implied by context). پیلی ژیان is a compound adjective‑noun: پیلی “elephantine/elephant‑colored” modifies ژیان “life/being,” producing a poetic tag meaning roughly “elephant‑lived” or “elephant‑like being.”
segments.s065-g20: بیاید and ببندد are third‑person singular present/subjunctive verbs used in sequence — here “(he/it) will come and (he/it) will bind/fasten.” بیاید can have an optative/impersonal force (“may come” / “will come” depending on tone); ببندد (from بستن) means “to bind/fasten/secure.” به مردی میان: به + مردی = toward/to a man (مردی indefinite), and میان “middle/midst,” so the phrase indicates binding/seizing into the middle among men (i.e. “in the midst of men”).
segments.s065-g21: This is an inverted, emphatic poetic order: جهان (the world) is the object/theme and زیرپای اندر آرد is the verb phrase “brings underfoot,” where زیرپای (زیرپا) = under the feet and اندر is the archaic prepositional particle meaning “in/within.” آرد is the present third‑person singular of آوردن “to bring.” به تیغ is an instrumental adjunct “with the sword.”
segments.s065-g22: نهد is the third‑person singular present of نهادن (to place/set). تخت شاه is the direct object “the king’s throne.” از بر پشت میغ is a compact classical phrase: از بر here functions as a prepositional cluster meaning roughly “upon/on,” پشت میغ = “the back (spine) of the blade.” The whole line means he will place the king’s throne upon the blade’s back — a vivid hyperbole.
segments.s065-g23: ببرد is a third‑person singular verbal form (classical/poetic) meaning “carried/took away” (the initial b- is the older narrative/prescriptive prefix often found in Ferdowsi). پی is an adverb here meaning “one after another / in succession,” placed after the verb for poetic word order. بدسگالان is the plural direct object “the ill‑fated / ill‑omened ones,” and ز خاک is a prepositional phrase “from the soil/earth” marking where they were taken from.
segments.s065-g24: به روی زمین is a locative phrase “on the face/surface of the earth” (به + روی + زمین). بر نماند is the verb phrase: بر here functions as ‘upon/on’ (emphatic locative before the verb) and نماند is the third‑person singular past (with the negative/ablative nuance here giving ‘did not remain / was not left’). مغاک, the noun placed after the verb (poetic word order), is the subject: “no chasm/abyss remained.”
segments.s065-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate temporal clause. برخاست is a past verb 'rose/awoke'; از خواب is a prepositional phrase 'from sleep' (خواب = sleep). با موبدان is a following prepositional phrase 'with the mobeds' that belongs to the same clause; the whole segment is a temporal clause that sets the time for the main action that follows.
segments.s065-g2: یکی here functions as an indefinite determiner 'a/one' (one meeting). انجمن کرد is a compound where انجمن (assembly/meeting) combines with the light verb کرد 'did/made' to mean 'held/convened (a meeting)'. با بخردان is a prepositional phrase 'with the wise (men)'; بخردان is the plural/collective form meaning 'the wise'.
segments.s065-g3: گشاد here is used poetically as a verbal/adjectival past-form meaning 'opened' or 'began' (i.e. 'he began/uttered'). آن سخن is the direct object 'that speech/discourse'. بر ستاره شمر is a topical/prepositional phrase 'about/on the star of Shamir' (بر used to mark the topic/subject-matter). The phrase thus means 'he began/spoke that speech about Shamir's star.'
segments.s065-g4: که introduces an indirect question/that-clause. فرجام این 'the outcome of this' (فرجام = end/outcome, این = this). بر چه باشد is an interrogative phrase 'what will be upon it/what will befall it' with باشد in the subjunctive/present sense used to ask about the (future) result. گذر at the end is a noun 'passage/occurrence' placed postpositively to indicate the matter under consideration ('this passing/occurrence'); overall: '...asking what the outcome of this occurrence will be.'
segments.s065-g5: دو گوهر 'two essences' (گوهر here = essence, not jewel). چو...چو is used as a comparative 'like/as' (چو آب و چو آتش = 'like water and like fire'), describing the two essences. به هم is an idiom meaning 'together' ('to one another'); this segment sets up the constituents that will be mixed.
segments.s065-g6: برآمیخته is a participial/adjectival-past form 'mingled/been mixed'. باشد is the present/subjunctive of 'to be' used here to express a conditional/possible state ('are mingled' / 'if they are mingled'). از بن ستم is a genitive-like phrase 'from the root of oppression' (از بن = from the root/foundation; ستم = injustice/oppression) indicating cause or source.
segments.s065-g7: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily/indeed.' که introduces the clause 'that there will be...'. باشد is the present/subjunctive 'will be' used here to assert an outcome. به‌روز شمار is a compound noun phrase functioning as 'the day of reckoning' or 'the appointed day' (به‌روز = 'on/of the day', شمار = 'count/reckoning'), together meaning 'the day of account/reckoning.'
segments.s065-g8: فریدون و ضحاک را uses را as the definite object/topic marker attached to the coordinated names 'Fereydun and Zahhak' (they are the parties affected). کارزار is the predicate noun 'battle/struggle'; the construction asserts that the day will be 'a battle for Fereydun and Zahhak' (i.e. a fight between them).
segments.s065-g33: خنک پادشاهی is a noun phrase where the adjective خنک ('pleasant/calm') precedes the noun پادشاهی ('kingship' or 'rule'). که introduces a relative/temporal clause; هنگام او ('in his time') completes that clause, with او referring back to the person whose reign is being described.
segments.s065-g34: زمانه is the subject (here meaning 'fate/fortune'); به شاهی برد is a past-tense verbal phrase (برد < بردن) meaning 'brought/raised to kingship' and takes نام او ('his name') as its object. Note: برد here functions as a past-tense verb 'brought/led', even though that precise sense is not listed in the provided senses.
segments.s065-g35: چو ب�شنید is a temporal clause ('when he heard'); چو = 'when', بشنید is the simple past 3rd-person of شنیدن ('he heard'). گفتار اخترشناس is the object phrase 'the astrologer's speech/words' with گفتار (speech) governed by the following noun اخترشناس ('astrologer') in a possessive/genitive juxtaposition.
segments.s065-g36: بخندید is past 3rd-person 'he laughed'; و simply coordinates the two actions; پذرفت is past 3rd-person of پذیرفتن 'he accepted'. ازیشان ('از' + شان) is a prepositional phrase 'from them', and سپاس is the noun 'thanks' — together 'he accepted thanks from them.'
segments.s065-g37: ببخشیدشان is past 3rd-person of بخشیدن with the enclitic شان ('to them'); in context it functions as 'he gave/bestowed on them' (classical بخشیدن can mean 'to give' as well as 'to forgive'). بی‌کران modifies زر و سیم ('boundless gold and silver') which is the object of the verb. Note: the provided sense list for ببخشیدشان did not include 'give/bestow', so that exact sense is not represented in the senses array.
segments.s065-g38: چو آرامش آمد is another temporal clause: چو = 'when', آرامش 'calm/peace', آمد past 'came'. به هنگام بیم is a temporal adverbial 'at the time/season of fear' (بیم = danger/fear), specifying when the giving took place.
segments.s065-g39: فرستاده زال ('Zāl's messenger') is the object; را is the direct-object marker that signals definiteness and precedes the verb in Persian word order. پیش خواند is a compound expression meaning 'he called/summoned (before him)' — خواند is past 'called/summoned' here and پیش indicates 'in the presence of/forward'.
segments.s065-g40: زهر گونه is an adverbial phrase 'in a poisonous/venomous manner' (زهر used figuratively = malice/venom, گونه = 'manner/way'). با او ('with him' / 'to him') indicates the addressee, سخنها ('words/speeches') is the object, and براند (past of راندن with prefixed ب-) means 'he drove away' or 'repelled' — so 'he repelled their words by speaking venomously to him.'
segments.s065-g41: بگفتش is a past reporting verb ('he said/told to him'), introducing a reported clause with the complementizer که. The clause که با او به خوبی بگوی is an instruction: با او ('to/with him') + به خوبی ('well/ kindly', an adverbial phrase formed with به) modifies بگوی, an imperative/subjunctive 3rd-person form meaning ‘speak/tell (him)’. Word order is classical: reporting verb → که + indirect object phrase + manner → verb.
segments.s065-g42: که introduces reported content ('that'). این آرزو را is the definite object ('this desire/wish') marked by را. نبد is a negative past/existential sense (classical form equivalent to 'نبود' — 'there was not / ought not to be'). هیـچ روی is an idiomatic adverbial phrase meaning 'in any way / on any account' (هیچ negates, روی = 'side/way').
segments.s065-g43: ولیکن is a conjunction 'but/yet'. چو here introduces a conditional ('if') and governs the noun phrase پیمان چنین بد نخست — literally 'if the covenant is thus bad at first/firstly' (چنین 'thus/so', بد 'bad', نخست 'at first'). Word order places the qualifying adverbs after the noun in a classical/poetic pattern.
segments.s065-g44: بهانه نشاید is an impersonal negative statement: نشاید 'it is not fitting/ought not' with بهانه ('an excuse/pretext') as the notional subject; جستن/جست ('to seek/use') follows with به بیداد ('to injustice/oppression'). Classical order places نشاید before the verb جست; idiomatically: 'one should not take an excuse to commit injustice.'
segments.s065-g45: من اینک is the subject + temporal 'I now'. به شبگیر is an adverbial time phrase ('at nightfall / toward night'); به here marks direction/time. ازین رزمگاه is a prepositional phrase 'from this battlefield' (از + این written here as از + ین). The classical/poetic sequence places time and source phrases before the verb.
segments.s065-g46: سوی شهر ایران is the goal 'toward the city of Iran' (سوی = toward). گذارم is a first-person verb form (classical/polite present/future sense 'I will send/bring/lead'); سپاه ('army') is the object. Classical word order places the goal phrase before the verb and the object after the verb.
segments.s065-g47: فرستاده را is the definite object 'the messenger' (را marks the object). داد is the past tense verb 'gave' and چندی درم is the amount 'a few dirhams' (چندی = some/a few, درم = dirhams). Classical order: object marker + object, verb, then the quantified direct object.
segments.s065-g48: بدو 'to him' introduces reported speech (to the messenger). گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (here treated as part of reported speech). خیره مزن is a negative imperative phrase: خیره 'staring/gaping' + مزن (negative imperative of زدن, 'do not do [it]') = 'do not gape/stare'. هیچ دم 'not for a moment' (هیچ 'not/none/ever' + دم 'moment') reinforces the prohibition.
segments.s065-g9: از اختر بجوئید و پاسخ دهید is two coordinated imperatives: از اختر (preposition + noun) is the source phrase 'from the star', followed by the imperative بجوئید ('seek', 2nd person plural). The conjunction و links a second imperative, پاسخ دهید ('give an answer', literally 'give a reply'). Word order is typical classical/poetic Persian: prepositional phrase + verb, with the verb forms in the plural imperative.
segments.s065-g10: همه کار و کردار فرخ نهید places the object phrase 'همه کار و کردار' ('all work and deeds') before the final verb/verb-form نهید. Here فرخ ('fortunate') qualifies the following idea, and نهید functions as a directive/verb meaning 'place/put/establish' (imperative or jussive sense). The order (object + modifiers + verb) is normal in older Persian poetry, so the clause reads 'place all work and conduct under/into good fortune.'
segments.s065-g11: ستاره‌شناسان به‌روز دراز is a noun (ستاره‌شناسان, 'astrologers/stargazers') followed by descriptive modifiers. به‌روز دراز here conveys prolonged experience or duration ('of long days' / 'long-practiced'), so the whole phrase means 'the astrologers who had long experience' or 'the seasoned astrologers.' The modifiers follow the noun, as common in Persian poetry.
segments.s065-g12: همی ز آسمان بازجستند راز is a full clause with the imperfective/continuous particle همی ('continually/keeps on') followed by the prepositional source phrase ز آسمان ('from the sky/heavens') and the past tense verb بازجستند (باز- + جستند, 'they sought again' / 'they searched'). راز is the direct object 'the secret/mystery.' Literal ordering is 'continually from the heavens they sought again the secret.'
segments.s065-g13: بدیدند و با خنده پیش آمدند contains two coordinated past-tense actions: بدیدند ('they saw') and آمدند ('they came') linked by و. The prepositional/adjunct phrase با خنده ('with laughter') modifies the second verb, and پیش ('forward/in front') adverbially marks the motion toward the listeners — 'they came forward, laughing.'
segments.s065-g14: که دو دشمن از بخت خویش آمدند is a subordinate/explanatory clause introduced by که ('that/for'), explaining why they approached. دو دشمن ('two enemies') is the subject, and از بخت خویش ('by their fortune' / 'through chance of fate') is an idiomatic circumstantial phrase; آمدند is the past-tense verb 'they came.' The word-by-word order shows the causal/explanatory nuance: 'for two enemies had come by their fate.'
segments.s065-g15: به سام نریمان ستاره شمر begins with the indirect object/prepositional phrase به سام نریمان ('to Sam Nārīmān'), then names the speaker as ستاره شمر ('the star Shamr' — i.e., Shamr the astrologer or the star-named Shamr). The sequence places the addressee before identification of the speaker, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s065-g16: چنین گفت کای گرد زرین کمر contains گفت ('said' — introducing direct speech) followed by a vocative clause کای ('O') and the descriptive phrase گرد زرین کمر. گرد here carries the sense 'round/about' or 'girth' used with کمر ('waist' / 'girdle') and زرین ('golden') modifies it: a poetic vocative 'O you with a golden girdle about your waist' (concisely 'O golden‑belted one').
segments.s065-g57: This is a simple past clause with subject first: 'سپهبد' (the spahbed) followed by an adverbial direction 'سوی شهر ایران' (toward the city of Iran) and the past-tense verb 'کشید' at the end. In classical Persian 'کشیدن' can mean 'to move/lead/advance' (here 'to march/bring'), so the verb takes the whole directional phrase as its complement.
segments.s065-g58: Here 'سپه را' (the army/host + object marker 'را') is the definite object; 'به نزد' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to/into the presence of' or 'up to'; 'دلیران' is the plural noun 'the brave ones'. The verb 'کشید' (past) closes the clause and again means 'drew/moved/brought' the army to those champions. The object marker 'را' emphasizes that 'سپه' is definite/specific.
segments.s065-g59: 'فرستاده' (the messenger) is the subject, followed by the past verb 'آمد' (came). 'دوان' is an adverbial/verbal adjective (running, rushing) modifying آمد — together 'آمد دوان' = 'came running'. 'سوی زال' is the directional phrase 'toward Zal'. This is a compact, poetic word order but follows Subject + (verb + adverb) + direction.
segments.s065-g60: This noun phrase describes what the messenger brought. 'ابا' here is a short, archaic/poetic particle introducing possession/quality (roughly 'bearing' or 'with') rather than its literal sense 'to refuse' in modern lists; it heads the phrase 'ابا بخت پیروز و فرخنده فال' = 'bearing a victorious and auspicious omen'. 'بخت' (fortune/lot) is qualified by the adjectives 'پیروز' (victorious) and 'فرخنده' (auspicious), and 'فال' is the head noun 'omen/augury'.
segments.s065-g61: The verb 'گرفت' (literally 'took') is used here in a poetic sense meaning 'uttered' or 'expressed' and introduces what follows. 'آفرین' is an exclamation/noun 'praise' or 'acclamation' and functions as the object of گرفت; 'زال' is the doer (subject) placed after the object for emphasis (inversion common in poetry). 'بر کردگار' is a prepositional phrase 'to/upon the Creator' marking the addressee of the praise.
segments.s065-g62: 'بران' is an adverbial/archaic particle meaning 'thereupon' or 'for that', linking the praise to its cause. 'بخشش' is a noun 'gift/grant' governed by 'بران', and 'گردش روزگار' is a genitive phrase 'the turning/cycle of fate' that specifies the nature of that gift. Together they mean 'for that grant — the turning of fortune'.
segments.s065-g63: This clause lists the coins and the act of giving: 'درم داد' (gave a dirham) with the verb 'داد' following the first noun; the conjunction 'و' links the second coin 'دینار', and the following noun phrase 'درویش را' marks the recipient with 'را' (here used in older/poetic usage to emphasize the definite recipient). The verb 'داد' therefore applies to both coin nouns.
segments.s065-g64: 'نوازنده شد' is a compound of the agent noun 'نوازنده' (one who soothes/entertains—poetically 'comforter' or 'musician') plus the past auxiliary 'شد' = 'became'. The following phrase 'مردم خویش را' ('his people' with the possessive clitic sh split as 'خوی' + 'ش' and 'را' for emphasis) names those affected; in this order the verb phrase comes before the object for poetic emphasis: 'he became a comforter to his people.'
segments.s065-g49: گسی (an indefinite 'a certain one' / 'someone') + کردش (past tense of کردن with the object clitic -ش = 'him') constructs an older/poetic clause where the verb comes with an enclitic object rather than an explicit pronoun (i.e. کرد + -ش = 'did/sent him'). The conjunction و links this clause to the next. خود is emphatic/reflexive 'himself', and به راه ایستاد is a fixed verbal phrase: ایستاد (past of ایستادن) here means 'set out on / took up the road' in this idiomatic/poetic use (not merely 'stood'); word order is subject/actor implied + verb, then an adverbial phrase به راه.
segments.s065-g50: سپاه و سپهبد form a compound subject ('the army and the spahbed'). از آن کار is a prepositional phrase 'because of / from that deed' (از with آن + noun), and شاد is a predicative adjective 'were glad' agreeing as the result/predicate. This is a simple past-state comment on the preceding action.
segments.s065-g51: ببستند is the past plural (they) of بستن with a duplicated initial b (poetic form) and here means 'bound/tied/captured'; the following از آن گرگساران is a partitive phrase 'of those wolf-people/br igands' (از ... indicating 'from/among'), and هزار (numeral) quantifies them — together giving 'they bound a thousand of those wolfish ones.' Note the verb-first classical word order and the partitive use of از.
segments.s065-g52: پیاده here indicates manner/place 'on foot' (how they were led or that the captives were foot-soldiers). به زاری is an adverbial phrase 'in lamentation / with wailing' (به + noun for manner). کشیدند is past plural 'they drew/led/dragged' (here transitive: 'they led [them]'), with the object omitted because it is understood from the previous clause (the thousand). خوار is a postpositive adjective 'despised / humbled', describing the state of those led away.
segments.s065-g53: دو بهره is a numeral + noun ('two portions' or 'two watches/parts'), and چو ... درگذشت is a temporal clause 'when ... had passed.' تیره شب is a genitive/attributive phrase 'dark night'; درگذشت (past of گذشتن/درگذشتن) here means 'passed by' (time passed), not 'died.' The clause sets the temporal frame for what follows.
segments.s065-g54: خروش سواران is a possessive/construct phrase 'the cry/roar of the horsemen' serving as subject. برآمد is a past intransitive verb 'arose / issued forth.' ز (poetic form of از) + دشت indicates origin 'from the plain.' Word order: subject + verb + adverbial 'from the plain.'
segments.s065-g55: همان is a demonstrative 'that same'; ناله کوس is a noun phrase 'the lament/cry of the drum' (کوس = war-drum). با introduces an instrument/ accompaniment 'with'; کره نای is a compound where کره (literally 'young/small') qualifies نای (the ney, reed-flute) — i.e. 'the small/young ney' or 'a little reed-flute.' This phrase lists the sounds that rose together.
segments.s065-g56: برآمد is 'arose/issued forth.' ز (poetic form of از) marks origin, followed by دهلیز پرده‌سرای — a genitive phrase 'the corridor (دهلیز) of the پرده‌سرا (stage-house/theatre)'. The phrase pinpoints the place from which the sound came.
segments.s065-g25: Not + X … not + Y construction: the first clause negates that something called سگسار remained, followed by a parallel negation for مازندران. ماند is the simple past/preterite stem (here 'remained/was left') placed after the subject (typical Persian verb-final order in poetry). سگسار is ambiguous/poetic; here it functions as a noun (rendered “the dishonoured” in this reading) and is parallel to the place-name مازندران.
segments.s065-g26: زمین را is the direct object (را marks the definite object); بشوید is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'become washed' / 'was washed' (here passive sense: the land is described as being washed). به introduces the instrument/means 'by/with' (به + instrument is common in poetry), and گرز گران is the instrument phrase 'heavy mace(s)' (گرز = mace, گران = heavy/weighty) placed after به.
segments.s065-g27: به خواب 'into sleep' (به + noun = into); اندرد is an older/poetic past form meaning 'entered' or 'came into' and is used with آرد ('brings') to personify an action (literally 'into sleep enters, brings'). آرد here is a present/habitual third-person form 'brings' (poetic usage). سر دردمند means 'the pained/aching head' (سر = head; دردمند = suffering), the direct object of آرد in sense of 'he brings the suffering head into sleep.'
segments.s065-g28: ببندد is the present/subjunctive or habitual-poetic third-person form of بستن 'to bind/close' (here 'will bind' or 'binds'). در جنگ و راه گزند is a compound locative: در جنگ 'in battle' and راه گزند 'the way/road of harm' (گزند = harm); the object bound (the head) is understood from the prior clause. The verb thus continues the prior action and locates it (he binds it for combat and for hazardous travel).
segments.s065-g29: بدو 'to him/unto him' is a short dative/benefactive phrase placed before the verb for emphasis; باشد is a subjunctive/poetic present of بودن 'to be' and functions here as an existential/linking verb ('is' / 'let there be'). ایرانیان را (Iranian + را) indicates the affected party ('for the Iranians' or 'the Iranians' hope') and امید is the predicate noun 'hope' — word order is topical (To him — there is — the Iranians' hope).
segments.s065-g30: ازو 'from him/of him' indicates source; پهلوان را with را marks 'the champion' as the affected party who receives or shows something; خرام here is best read as a nominal 'proud gait/strut' (a stately stride) rather than a verb. و links that noun to نوید 'tidings/good news' — literally: 'from him, the champion's proud gait and good news' (i.e., the champion derives swagger and encouragement from him).
segments.s065-g31: پی باره‌ای 'after a certain occasion' — پی here means 'after/following' and باره‌ای 'an instance/occasion' (the indefinite -ای gives 'a certain'); کو is an archaic relative 'which/that' (here introducing the clause), and چماند is an older past-form 'remained/was left' — together: 'after an occasion which remained in battle' (i.e., after one encounter in the fighting). به جنگ localizes the occasion 'in battle.'
segments.s065-g32: بمالد is the present/subjunctive/poetic form of مالیدن 'to smear/anoint' (here 'he smeared' or 'he smears'). The short form برو is irregular in the lemma list; in this phrase it functions as an archaic contraction of بر او 'upon him' (poetic compression), i.e. 'on him / on his.' روی is 'face' (the surface receiving the smear). جنگی is an adjective 'warlike' and پلنگ 'leopard' is the simile: 'he smeared (it) on his face like a war-leopard.' Because برو is used poetically as 'on him,' its listed literal senses ('go') do not apply here.

sentences.s065-s9: I bring you the glad tidings about the daughter of Mehrab and Zal: that the two of them will be matched together in joy.
sentences.s065-s10: From these two skillful ones an elephant‑like life will come — (he) will come and bind (it) in the midst of men.
sentences.s065-s11: He will bring the world underfoot with the sword; he will set the king’s throne on the back of the blade.
sentences.s065-s12: One by one the ill‑fated were swept away from the soil; on the face of the earth no chasm remained.
sentences.s065-s1: When he rose from sleep, he held a meeting with the mobeds and the wise.
sentences.s065-s2: He opened that discourse concerning Shamr's star, asking what the outcome of this matter would be.
sentences.s065-s3: If two essences, like water and like fire, are mingled together from the root by oppression.
sentences.s065-s4: Verily, that will be the day of reckoning — a battle between Fereydun and Zahhak.
sentences.s065-s17: How fortunate the rule was that, in his time, fate raised his name to kingship.
sentences.s065-s18: When he heard the astrologer's words, he laughed and accepted their thanks.
sentences.s065-s19: He bestowed on them boundless gold and silver when calm arrived in the hour of fear.
sentences.s065-s20: He summoned Zāl's messenger before him; speaking to him in a venomous manner, he drove off their words.
sentences.s065-s21: He told him to tell him gently that this wish ought not to exist in any way.
sentences.s065-s22: But if a covenant is thus bad in the first place, it is not fitting to use that as an excuse to resort to injustice.
sentences.s065-s23: I now, at nightfall, will lead the army from this battlefield toward the city of Iran.
sentences.s065-s24: He gave the messenger a few dirhams and said to him, 'Do not stare even for a moment.'
sentences.s065-s5: Consult the star and give an answer; place all work and conduct under good fortune.
sentences.s065-s6: The long-practiced astrologers continually sought the secret from the sky.
sentences.s065-s7: They saw and, with laughter, came forward, for two enemies had come by their fate.
sentences.s065-s8: To Sam Nærīmān the star Shamr said thus: 'O girded with a golden belt (i.e. O golden-belted one)...'
sentences.s065-s29: The spahbed marched toward the city of Iran; he brought the host before the brave men.
sentences.s065-s30: A messenger came running to Zal, bearing a victorious and auspicious omen.
sentences.s065-s31: Zal uttered praise to the Creator for that gift — the turning of fortune.
sentences.s065-s32: He gave a dirham and a dinar to the dervish, and became a comforter to his people.
sentences.s065-s25: He sent him off and himself set out on the road; the army and the spahbed rejoiced at that deed.
sentences.s065-s26: They bound a thousand of those wolfish men; they led them away on foot in lamentation, despised.
sentences.s065-s27: When two watches (two portions) of the dark night had passed, the cry of the horsemen rose from the plain.
sentences.s065-s28: That same cry of the drum, together with the small ney (reed-flute), rose from the corridor of the stage-house.
sentences.s065-s13: Neither the dishonoured remained nor Mazandaran; the land was washed by heavy maces.
sentences.s065-s14: He brings the aching head into sleep; he will bind it in battle and on the road of harm.
sentences.s065-s15: He is the Iranians' hope; from him the champion takes proud stride and tidings.
sentences.s065-s16: After a certain occasion that remained in battle, he smeared it on his face like a war‑leopard.
